ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for stir-fried pepper and salt squid; closest parent 'stir-fried food' (FOODON_00003446) is used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for stir-fried pepper and salt squid; 'Squid and cuttlefish' (A00KY) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT does not have a specific code for stir-fried squid; 'Fried food' (226530009) is the closest parent.

allergens: gluten (if flour or starch coating contains wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried pepper and spiced salt squid, often served as a main dish or shared appetizer, is approximately 180 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, fried, breaded (FDC ID 172159)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data is estimated from 'Squid, fried, breaded' (FDC ID 172159) from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, the closest available approved source for fried squid. The preparation 'stir-fried pepper and spiced salt' implies a lighter coating than 'breaded', so values represent a reasonable upper-bound estimate for macronutrients. No direct Singapore-specific or HPB Foodo entry for this dish was identified.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed protein-starch foods — analogous estimate based on lightly coated fried squid
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for stir-fried pepper and salt squid exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 50 (low) based on the food's composition: squid is a very low-carbohydrate protein source, and the light flour or starch coating contributes minimal glycemic carbohydrates. This is analogous to other lightly coated fried protein foods with low-to-moderate starch content, which consistently fall in the low GI range (GI ≤ 55) per the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Glycemic load per 180 g serving calculated as (50 × 9.2 × 1.80) / 100 = 8.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spiced salt seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume this dish in moderation, as the spiced salt seasoning contributes approximately 370 mg sodium per 100 g, which can meaningfully add to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content from light starch coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low estimated glycemic index of 50 and a moderate carbohydrate content of approximately 9 g per 100 g, making it a suitable protein-centred option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content from squid and added fat from stir-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is naturally high in cholesterol (approximately 233 mg per 100 g) and the stir-frying process adds fat including saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir Fried Pepper and Spiced Salt Squid is a popular Chinese seafood dish widely served in Singaporean restaurants and zi char stalls. The dish features fresh squid pieces lightly dusted with flour or cornstarch, then rapidly stir-fried in a wok over high heat with a fragrant blend of ground black pepper, coarse salt, and sometimes five-spice powder or chilli. The high-heat wok cooking produces a crispy, golden exterior while keeping the squid interior tender and succulent, delivering the characteristic 'wok hei' smoky aroma. The flavour profile is savoury, aromatic, and mildly spicy. Nutritionally, the dish provides a good source of protein (approximately 18 g per 100 g) and is rich in selenium and vitamin B12 from the squid. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the spiced salt seasoning, making portion control important for those managing hypertension. The low glycemic index makes it suitable for blood sugar management, though the cholesterol content warrants moderation for cardiovascular health. It is commonly served as a shared main course or appetizer at Chinese seafood restaurants.
common_user_queries: How to make stir-fried salt and pepper squid?
Nutritional value of salt and pepper squid
